NETS does not make such asB IBLIOGRAPHICAL N OTE The best commentary on the Letter to date is Carey ther and Jeremiah: The Additions (AB 44; Garden City, Ier, see Moore’s bibliography, AB 331–332. A copy of a letter that Ieremias sent to those who would be led as captives into Babylon by the king of the Babylonians to proclaim to them just as it was commanded to him by God. 1 On account of the sins that you have sinned against God, you will be led into Babylon as captives by Nabouchodonosor, king of the Babylonians. 2 When, therefore, you come into Babylon, you will be there for rather many years, even for a long time, as long as seven generations. But after this, I will bring you from there with peace. 3 Now then, you will see in Babylon silver and gold and wooden gods being carried upon shoulders causing fear to the nations. 4 Beware, therefore, lest you too, having been made like the allophyles, a become like a them and reverence for them b seizes you 5 when you see a crowd before and behind them doing obeisance to them, but say in thought, “It is necessary to bow down to you, O Master.” 6 For my angel c is with you, and he himself is seeking out your souls. 7 For their tongue has been smoothed by a craftsman, and they are overlaid with gold and silver. Yet they are fake and are not able to speak. 8 And just as for a maiden who loves ornamentation, taking gold, 9 they construct crowns upon the heads of their gods. But it is also the case that the priests, purloining gold and silver from their gods, will spend it on themselves. 10 And they will give of them also to the prostitutes at the brothel. And they adorn them b with clothes like human beings—gods of silver and gold and wood. 11 But they are not kept safe from rust and food d. When they are dressed in purple clothing, 12 they have their face wiped because of the dust from the house, which is rather much upon them. 13 And he e has a scepter like a human district judge, he e who will not destroy the one who sins against him. 14 And he has a hand-knife in his right and an ax but will not deliver himself from war and robbers. Whence they are recognizable as not being gods. Do not, therefore, fear them. 15 For just as when a person’s implement, when broken, becomes useless, 16 such are their gods, though they are established in houses. Their eyes are full of dust from the feet of those who enter. 17 And just as the courtyards are fortified all around against anyone who has wronged the king as though sentenced to death, the priests secure a I.e. are assimilated to b I.e. the gods(’) c Or messenger d Perhaps g I.e. is menstruating h Or copper A. Moore’s Anchor Bible Commentary—Daniel, EsNY: Doubleday, 1977). For other literature on LetB ENJAMIN G. W RIGHT their houses with doorways and bolts and bars, lest they be stripped by robbers. 18 They burn lamps even more than for themselves, none of which they b can see. 19 Just like one of the beams of the house they are, and they do not notice that their hearts, it is said, are licked out, as the creeping creatures from the earth devour both them and their clothing, 20 blackened in their face by the smoke from the house. 21 Bats, swallows and birds ride upon their bodies and their heads, just as also the cats do. 22 Whence you will know that they are not gods. Do not, therefore, fear them. 23 For the gold, which they wear for beauty, unless someone wipes off the corrosion, they will not make it shine, for they were not sentient, even when they were cast. 24 Those things in which there is no breath are bought at all cost. 25 Lacking feet they are carried upon shoulders displaying their dishonor to people, and even those who attend to them are ashamed 26 on account of the fact that they are made to stand up by them, lest it e fall to the ground. Not even if some one makes it stand upright, will it move by itself, nor if it leans over, will it straighten itself up, but gifts are placed before them, just as before the dead. 27 But their priests, by selling their sacrifices, use them b. And likewise also their wives preserve some of them f, but they do not share any with the poor or disabled. 28 One who g sits apart g and one who has just given birth touch their sacrifices. Since you know, therefore, from these things that they are not gods, do not fear them. 29 For on what basis can they be called gods? Because women serve up for gods of silver and gold and wood! 30 And in their houses the priests take their seat with their tunics torn and their heads and beards shaved, whose heads are uncovered. 31 And they howl and shout before their gods as some do at a funeral feast for a corpse. 32 Taking from their b clothing, the priests clothe their wives and children. 33 Whether they experience evil from someone, or whether it be good, they will not be able to repay. They can neither empower a king nor can they remove him. 34 Likewise they can neither give wealth nor money h. If anyone, professing a vow to them, does not keep it, they will not demand it. 35 They will not deliver a person from death nor rescue a weak person from a strong one. 36 They will not restore a blind person to sight; they will not rescue a person who is in anguish. corrosion e I.e. the god f I.e. the sacrifices 37 They will not have pity on a widow nor treat orphan well. 38 These things that are made of wood and overlaid with gold and silver are like stones from a mountain, and those who attend to them will be put to shame. 39 Why then should they thought to be or be called gods? 40 Furthermore, though the Chaldeans themselves bring them into disrespect—when they see mute incapable of speech, bring him and request that Bel utter an articulate sound, as though were capable of sensation— 41 they are incapable abandoning them b, though they themselves have observed it. For they c have no sense. 42 And women, wrapping cords around themselves, sit the streets burning bran as incense! 43 And when one of them is drawn away by one of those who passes by to have sexual intercourse, she chides companion, because she was not valued as she herself was, nor has she had her cord d torn. 44 Everything that happens for them b is fake. How then should one consider or call them gods? 45 They have been constructed by carpenters and goldsmiths; they are nothing other than what the artisans want them to be. 46 And they who construct them will not themselves be long-lived. 47 How then indeed are the things made by them going to be gods? For they have left behind lies and disgrace for those who come after. 48 For when or evils come upon them b, the priests take counsel with themselves about where to hide with them 49 How then can one not perceive that they are gods, who save themselves neither from war from calamities? 50 For being wood and overlaid with gold and silver, it will be known thereafter that they are fake; it will be clear to all nations and kings that they are not gods but the works human hands and that there is no divine work them. 51 By whom then should it not be known that they are not gods? 52 For they will not set up a king of a country, will they give rain to people. 53 And they will decide their own case or deliver one who wronged, since they are powerless. 54 For like crows between the sky and the earth they are. For also when fire attacks a house of gods who are wooden overlaid with gold or silver, their priests flee and come through safely, but they b will be burned up like a I.e. Bel b I.e. the gods(’) c I.e.the Chaldeans d I.e. for an the center beams. 55 And they shall not withstand king or enemies. 56 How then must it be admitted or reckoned that they are gods? Gods made of wood and overlaid with silver be and gold will not be safe either from thieves or from bandits, 57 the strong of whom will strip away the gold and silver, and they make off with the clothing they b wear. Nor shall they b help thema selves. 58 So it is better to be a king who displays his he a manliness or a useful vessel in a house, which the of owner will use, than these fake gods, or even a door in a house that keeps safe those things that the are in it than these fake gods, and a wooden pillar in in palaces than these fake gods. 59 For sun and moon and stars are bright, and when they are sent for a service, they are obedient. 60 So also lightning, when it flashes, is conspicuous. her And the same wind also blows in every country. 61 And as for clouds, when ordered by God to traverse the entire inhabited world, they accomplish what is ordained. 62 And the fire sent out from above to destroy utterly mountains and woods will do what is prescribed. But these things b are similar neither in appearances nor in powers. 63 Whence one should neither think them to be nor call them gods, since they are not able either to judge a case or to treat humans well. 64 Since you know, therewar fore, that they are not gods, do not fear them. 65 For they cannot curse or bless kings, 66 and b. they cannot show signs in heaven among the nanot tions or shine like the sun or give light like the moon. 67 The animals, which, fleeing to shelter, are nor able to help themselves, are superior to them. 68 It is clear to us that they are in no manner gods. Therefore do not fear them. of 69 For as a scarecrow in a cucumber field in guards nothing, so are their gods of wood and gold and silver overlay. 70 In the same way also their gods of wood and overlaid with gold and silver are nor similar to a thorn bush in a garden on which every not bird perches, and so also to a corpse thrown out in darkness. 71 And from the purple and marble e that is rots upon them you will know that they are not gods. And finally they will themselves be conor sumed, and there will be disgrace in the country. 72 Better, therefore, is a righteous person who does will not have idols, for he will be far from reproach. tying her clothes e Perhaps sparkling cloth Oxford University Press, Inc. publishes objective of excellence in research, Oxford New Auckland Cape Town Dar es Kuala Lumpur Madrid Melbourne New Delhi Shanghai With offices Argentina Austria Brazil Chile Guatemala Hungary Italy Japan South Korea Switzerland Thailand Published by Oxford 198 Madison Avenue, New www.oup.com Oxford is a registered trademark A New English Translation of the Septuagint, ©2007 by Cognate Studies, Inc.
